title: Describing My World
focus: Adjectives, colors and sizes, make comparisons between things.
function: Describe and compare things with detail
grammar: Adjective before noun: a big red book
Comparatives: bigger than, more expensive than
Superlatives: the biggest, the most important
foundationalLiteracy: The -er and -est endings as a spelling pattern, including the doubling rule (big → bigger). Recognizing that longer adjectives take more instead.
culturalNote: Comparison is the first genuinely academic language function in the sequence — it is what science labs, math word problems, and social studies all run on. Point this out to students explicitly: this unit is the one that starts paying off in their other classes. Avoid comparisons of people's appearance, wealth, or home countries; keep the objects neutral.

1.canDo: Point to an object when it is described by one attribute.
1.scaffold: Object sets varying by a single feature: the big ball, the red ball.
1.check: Selects the correct object from 4 in 8 of 10 trials.
1.l1Bridge: Adjectives glossed with paired objects; teach opposites together.

2.canDo: Understand a spoken comparison of two things.
2.scaffold: Paired picture cards. Comparison sentences limited to taught structures.
2.check: Identifies which of two items is described in 4 of 5 comparisons.
2.l1Bridge: Explain comparison structure in the home language — languages mark comparison very differently.

3.canDo: Follow a spoken description of several items and rank them.
3.scaffold: Ranking organizer. Description at normal rate, played twice.
3.check: Correctly orders 4 items from a spoken description using superlatives.

1.canDo: Describe an object with one adjective.
1.scaffold: Adjective picture bank. Two-word answers accepted: big book.
1.check: Describes 6 objects with an appropriate adjective.
1.l1Bridge: Student may give the adjective in the home language; teacher supplies the English.

2.canDo: Compare two things using a comparative frame.
2.scaffold: Frame: The ___ is ___er than the ___. Object pairs provided.
2.check: Produces 4 correct comparative sentences about real objects.
2.l1Bridge: Explicitly contrast how the home language forms comparatives with English -er / more.

3.canDo: Compare several things and justify which is best for a purpose.
3.scaffold: Superlative bank plus a because frame. Real decision scenario provided.
3.check: Uses a superlative and justifies the choice with a because clause.
3.l1Bridge: Justify the choice in the home language first, then in English with because.

1.scaffold: Word–picture cards with opposite pairs shown together.

2.canDo: Read a description and identify which object it refers to.
2.scaffold: 3-sentence descriptions with 4 candidate pictures. Adjectives bolded.
2.check: Correctly identifies the object in 4 of 5 descriptions.

3.canDo: Read a comparison text and extract the criteria being compared.
3.scaffold: Short product or animal comparison plus a criteria table to complete.
3.check: Fills a 2-item, 3-criteria comparison table from a paragraph.
3.l1Bridge: Criteria table headings glossed; the table is completed in English.

1.scaffold: Picture page with an adjective word bank and labeling lines.

2.canDo: Write comparative sentences about two objects.
2.scaffold: Frames plus an adjective bank marked for -er versus more.
2.check: Writes 4 comparative sentences with correct -er/more choice.
2.l1Bridge: Mark the -er / more split explicitly against the home-language pattern.

3.canDo: Write a paragraph comparing two things and stating a preference.
3.scaffold: Comparison organizer with criteria columns. Transition bank: however, but, while.
3.check: Writes a 6-sentence comparison with 2 criteria, a transition, and a justified preference.
3.l1Bridge: Plan the comparison in the home language, write it in English.

1.scaffold: Holds up or points to the item that matches the adjective the teacher says. Production is one word; the comparison is made physically.

2.scaffold: Single comparative frame with both objects physically present on the desk.

3.say: Both [object] and [object] are [shared quality], but the [object] is [comparative] because [reason]. I think the [object] is the [superlative] for [purpose].
3.scaffold: Open frame that names a shared quality first, then a difference, then a purpose-based judgment. This is the structure of a lab conclusion and a text-comparison essay.
